item.title: Azi-Vec: High-resolution Azimuth-sensitive PolSAR image generation for Vehicle automatic target recognition
item.dc:creator: Yuzhen Xie
Huaiyue Ding
Ye Liu
Nengcai Li
Deliang Xiang
Yi Su
item.prism:publicationName: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing
item.description: High-resolution polarimetric synthetic aperture radar (PolSAR) has clear advantages for automatic target recognition (ATR) by capturing fine-grained scattering from ground objects. However, polarimetric responses vary significantly with the relative azimuth between the radar line of sight and the target. This sensitivity leads to multi-view feature inconsistency and feature confusion, which together degrade the accuracy and generalization of ATR models. We propose a multi-azimuth data generation method specifically designed for PolSAR vehicle targets. By using measured PolSAR data from two sampled azimuths as input, the method constructs a geometry-aware, azimuth-coupled sparse representation of polarimetric features. This representation serves as a prior to synthesize target images at arbitrary intermediate azimuths within the same vehicle class. Central to this approach is an azimuth-sensitive feature space that jointly models geometric edges, polarimetric cues, and imaging parameters. A physically constrained framework further enforces radar imaging geometry and scattering-structure consistency to simplify the learning of complex mappings. Experiments under standard operating conditions (SOC) and extended operating conditions (EOC) show that the generated images accurately reproduce key polarimetric scattering characteristics and geometric structures while adhering to PolSAR imaging principles. Compared with existing state-of-the-art methods including CVAE-GAN, ACGAN, ATGAN, and SD, our approach achieves superior performance, yielding an FID of 48.2844, a KID of 0.0135, an SSIM of 0.5058, and an FSIM of 0.7186. Vehicle ATR experiments further confirm the utility of the generated data. Replacing a portion of real training samples with synthetic ones results in accuracy decreases of only 0.55%, 0.94%, and 0.86% for VGG16, ResNet50, and a PolSAR recognition model, respectively. The dataset and code will be available at https://github.com/rainz-x/PolSAR-generation/tree/main . Published: 2026-03-28T08:31:07+00:00 Venue: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing Score: 0.817 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yuzhen Xie; Huaiyue Ding; Ye Liu; Nengcai Li; Deliang Xiang; Yi Su

Journal: ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing

DOI: 10.1016/j.isprsjprs.2026.03.038

Relevance: 0.817 (must_read)

High-resolution polarimetric synthetic aperture radar (PolSAR) has clear advantages for automatic target recognition (ATR) by capturing fine-grained scattering from ground objects. However, polarimetric responses vary significantly with the relative azimuth between the radar line of sight and the target. This sensitivity leads to multi-view feature inconsistency and feature confusion, which together degrade the accuracy and generalization of ATR models. We propose a multi-azimuth data generation method specifically designed for PolSAR vehicle targets. By using measured PolSAR data from two sampled azimuths as input, the method constructs a geometry-aware, azimuth-coupled sparse representation of polarimetric features. This representation serves as a prior to synthesize target images at arbitrary intermediate azimuths within the same vehicle class. Central to this approach is an azimuth-sensitive feature space that jointly models geometric edges, polarimetric cues, and imaging parameters. A physically constrained framework further enforces radar imaging geometry and scattering-structure consistency to simplify the learning of complex mappings. Experiments under standard operating conditions (SOC) and extended operating conditions (EOC) show that the generated images accurately reproduce key polarimetric scattering characteristics and geometric structures while adhering to PolSAR imaging principles. Compared with existing state-of-the-art methods including CVAE-GAN, ACGAN, ATGAN, and SD, our approach achieves superior performance, yielding an FID of 48.2844, a KID of 0.0135, an SSIM of 0.5058, and an FSIM of 0.7186. Vehicle ATR experiments further confirm the utility of the generated data. Replacing a portion of real training samples with synthetic ones results in accuracy decreases of only 0.55%, 0.94%, and 0.86% for VGG16, ResNet50, and a PolSAR recognition model, respectively. The dataset and code will be available at https://github.com/rainz-x/PolSAR-generation/tree/main .

item.title: Harnessing the Power of Local Representations for Few-Shot Classification
item.dc:creator: Shi Tang
Guiming Luo
Xinchen Ye
Zhiyi Xia
item.description: Generalization to unseen classes is a central challenge in few-shot classification. While recent metric-based methods leverage local representations to address this, their effectiveness is often limited by two key issues: (i) inadequate supervision during feature extractor pretraining, and (ii) inflexible similarity metrics that struggle with diverse local feature set compositions. In this work, we fully harness the potential of local representations to overcome these limitations. First, we introduce a novel pretraining paradigm for the feature extractor that learns from randomly cropped image patches using soft labels. This approach captures class-level diversity while mitigating semantic misalignment from hard labels. To align network outputs with these soft labels, we propose the UniCon KL-Divergence loss, ensuring balanced contributions from all base classes for “non-base” patches. Second, for the similarity metric, we formulate the matching of local feature sets as an entropy-regularized optimal transport problem, effectively handling homogeneous sets. We further imbue the metric with adaptability through a dedicated Modulation Module. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on three widely-used benchmarks, notably surpassing both transductive and cross-modal approaches in fine-grained classification scenarios. Published: 2026-03-28T07:26:26+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.816 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Shi Tang; Guiming Luo; Xinchen Ye; Zhiyi Xia

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.113597

Relevance: 0.816 (must_read)

Generalization to unseen classes is a central challenge in few-shot classification. While recent metric-based methods leverage local representations to address this, their effectiveness is often limited by two key issues: (i) inadequate supervision during feature extractor pretraining, and (ii) inflexible similarity metrics that struggle with diverse local feature set compositions. In this work, we fully harness the potential of local representations to overcome these limitations. First, we introduce a novel pretraining paradigm for the feature extractor that learns from randomly cropped image patches using soft labels. This approach captures class-level diversity while mitigating semantic misalignment from hard labels. To align network outputs with these soft labels, we propose the UniCon KL-Divergence loss, ensuring balanced contributions from all base classes for “non-base” patches. Second, for the similarity metric, we formulate the matching of local feature sets as an entropy-regularized optimal transport problem, effectively handling homogeneous sets. We further imbue the metric with adaptability through a dedicated Modulation Module. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on three widely-used benchmarks, notably surpassing both transductive and cross-modal approaches in fine-grained classification scenarios.

item.title: EdgeFusionNet: Edge Information-Guided Small Object Detection for Remote Sensing Images
item.dc:creator: Linsong Xiao
Hanyun Li
Sai Yao
Chengyu Hou
item.description: In small object detection tasks for remote sensing images, the edge details and spatial location information of small objects are significantly degraded in deep feature maps due to successive downsampling operations in deep convolutional neural networks, resulting in reduced detection accuracy. To improve the accuracy of small object detection, this paper proposes EdgeFusionNet, a novel framework that, unlike existing methods relying on implicit multi-scale fusion, explicitly extracts and enhances boundary-relevant edge information from shallow layers and adaptively injects it into deep semantic features. First, an Edge Information Extractor (EIE) is designed, which utilizes the Sobel operator and Edge-Guided Attention (EGA) to extract edge information from shallow layers. Subsequently, an EdgeFusion structure is proposed to integrate edge information into deeper feature maps. Finally, feature fusion and interaction are performed through ScaleFusion Blocks before producing the output via the decoder. Experimental results on the VisDrone2019, HIT-UAV and RSOD datasets demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms other state-of-the-art approaches. Experimental results on three public datasets demonstrate that EdgeFusionNet achieves mAP 50 of 44.1%, 95.2%, and 97.1% respectively, with improvements of 4.3%, 1.3%, and 4.4% in mAP 50 compared to DEIM, while maintaining real-time inference capability. Published: 2026-03-29T06:55:35+00:00 Venue: Pattern Recognition Score: 0.810 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Linsong Xiao; Hanyun Li; Sai Yao; Chengyu Hou

Journal: Pattern Recognition

DOI: 10.1016/j.patcog.2026.113621

Relevance: 0.810 (must_read)

In small object detection tasks for remote sensing images, the edge details and spatial location information of small objects are significantly degraded in deep feature maps due to successive downsampling operations in deep convolutional neural networks, resulting in reduced detection accuracy. To improve the accuracy of small object detection, this paper proposes EdgeFusionNet, a novel framework that, unlike existing methods relying on implicit multi-scale fusion, explicitly extracts and enhances boundary-relevant edge information from shallow layers and adaptively injects it into deep semantic features. First, an Edge Information Extractor (EIE) is designed, which utilizes the Sobel operator and Edge-Guided Attention (EGA) to extract edge information from shallow layers. Subsequently, an EdgeFusion structure is proposed to integrate edge information into deeper feature maps. Finally, feature fusion and interaction are performed through ScaleFusion Blocks before producing the output via the decoder. Experimental results on the VisDrone2019, HIT-UAV and RSOD datasets demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms other state-of-the-art approaches. Experimental results on three public datasets demonstrate that EdgeFusionNet achieves mAP 50 of 44.1%, 95.2%, and 97.1% respectively, with improvements of 4.3%, 1.3%, and 4.4% in mAP 50 compared to DEIM, while maintaining real-time inference capability.

item.title: Learning from the Waist: Aspect-Ratio-Aware Positive Sampling for Oriented Ship Detection
item.dc:creator: Dandan Ma
Hao Huang
Zhiyu Jiang
item.description: Ship detection in earth observation imagery has become a key research focus for maritime resource monitoring and environmental management applications. However, the random orientations, large aspect ratios, and substantial scale variations of ships pose significant challenges for accurate localization and classification. To tackle these challenges, we propose an aspect-ratio-aware one-stage oriented ship detection method with dynamic label assignment. Our approach incorporates three novel strategies: (1) Trigonometric Normalized Coder (TNC), which employs trigonometric functions with normalization to create a concise angle encoder-decoder that effectively resolves angular boundary discontinuity; (2) Ratio-Driven Dynamic Assignment (RDDA), which uniquely utilizes ship aspect ratios to adaptively adjust positive sample selection, particularly incorporating high-quality features from the waist of ship that conventional methods overlook, further enhanced by a two-dimensional Gaussian distribution to measure sample quality; and (3) Attention-Guided Feature Fusion Module (AGFFM), which strengthens multi-scale feature representation by applying spatial and channel attention mechanisms across feature pyramid levels while effectively fusing adjacent layers’ features. Extensive experiments on three public datasets (HRSC2016, HRSID, and SSDD+) demonstrate that our approach achieves superior localization and classification performance compared to state-of-the-art one-stage detectors, especially for ships with extreme aspect ratios and varying scales. Published: 2026-03-28T16:12:54+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.809 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Dandan Ma; Hao Huang; Zhiyu Jiang

Journal: Expert Systems with Applications

DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2026.132251

Relevance: 0.809 (must_read)

Ship detection in earth observation imagery has become a key research focus for maritime resource monitoring and environmental management applications. However, the random orientations, large aspect ratios, and substantial scale variations of ships pose significant challenges for accurate localization and classification. To tackle these challenges, we propose an aspect-ratio-aware one-stage oriented ship detection method with dynamic label assignment. Our approach incorporates three novel strategies: (1) Trigonometric Normalized Coder (TNC), which employs trigonometric functions with normalization to create a concise angle encoder-decoder that effectively resolves angular boundary discontinuity; (2) Ratio-Driven Dynamic Assignment (RDDA), which uniquely utilizes ship aspect ratios to adaptively adjust positive sample selection, particularly incorporating high-quality features from the waist of ship that conventional methods overlook, further enhanced by a two-dimensional Gaussian distribution to measure sample quality; and (3) Attention-Guided Feature Fusion Module (AGFFM), which strengthens multi-scale feature representation by applying spatial and channel attention mechanisms across feature pyramid levels while effectively fusing adjacent layers’ features. Extensive experiments on three public datasets (HRSC2016, HRSID, and SSDD+) demonstrate that our approach achieves superior localization and classification performance compared to state-of-the-art one-stage detectors, especially for ships with extreme aspect ratios and varying scales.

item.title: FSGNet: A Frequency-Aware and Semantic Guidance Network for Infrared Small Target Detection
item.dc:creator: Yingmei Zhang
Wangtao Bao
Yong Yang
Weiguo Wan
Qin Xiao
Xueting Zou
item.description: Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) aims to identify and distinguish small targets from complex backgrounds. Leveraging the powerful multi-scale feature fusion capability of the U-Net architecture, IRSTD has achieved significant progress. However, U-Net suffers from semantic degradation when transferring high-level features from deep to shallow layers, limiting the precise localization of small targets. To address this issue, this paper proposes FSGNet, a lightweight and effective detection framework incorporating frequency-aware and semantic guidance mechanisms. Specifically, a multi-directional interactive attention module is proposed throughout the encoder to capture fine-grained and directional features, enhancing the network's sensitivity to small, low-contrast targets. To suppress background interference propagated through skip connections, a multi-scale frequency-aware module leverages Fast Fourier transform to filter out target-similar clutter while preserving salient target structures. At the deepest layer, a global pooling module captures high-level semantic information, which is subsequently upsampled and propagated to each decoder stage through the global semantic guidance flows, ensuring semantic consistency and precise localization across scales. Extensive experiments on four public IRSTD datasets demonstrate that FSGNet achieves superior detection performance and maintains high efficiency, highlighting its practical applicability and robustness. The codes will be released on https://github.com/Wangtao-Bao/FSGNet. Published: 2026-03-26T12:38:29+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.808 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yingmei Zhang; Wangtao Bao; Yong Yang; Weiguo Wan; Qin Xiao; Xueting Zou

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.808 (must_read)

Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) aims to identify and distinguish small targets from complex backgrounds. Leveraging the powerful multi-scale feature fusion capability of the U-Net architecture, IRSTD has achieved significant progress. However, U-Net suffers from semantic degradation when transferring high-level features from deep to shallow layers, limiting the precise localization of small targets. To address this issue, this paper proposes FSGNet, a lightweight and effective detection framework incorporating frequency-aware and semantic guidance mechanisms. Specifically, a multi-directional interactive attention module is proposed throughout the encoder to capture fine-grained and directional features, enhancing the network's sensitivity to small, low-contrast targets. To suppress background interference propagated through skip connections, a multi-scale frequency-aware module leverages Fast Fourier transform to filter out target-similar clutter while preserving salient target structures. At the deepest layer, a global pooling module captures high-level semantic information, which is subsequently upsampled and propagated to each decoder stage through the global semantic guidance flows, ensuring semantic consistency and precise localization across scales. Extensive experiments on four public IRSTD datasets demonstrate that FSGNet achieves superior detection performance and maintains high efficiency, highlighting its practical applicability and robustness. The codes will be released on https://github.com/Wangtao-Bao/FSGNet.

item.title: LYA-YOLO: A lightweight and accurate YOLO model in drone aerial image scenes
item.dc:creator: Pengwei Wu
Yunfeng Xu
Yujie Ma
Yan Zhang
Yunling Xu
item.description: Advancements in unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) have significantly expanded their application domains, encompassing urban surveillance, intelligent transportation, and infrastructure inspection. However, existing UAV-based object detection models still suffer from limitations such as low accuracy in small object detection, constrained real-time performance, and high computational costs. These constraints hinder their deployment capabilities on resource-constrained devices and their real-time detection abilities. To address these limitations, this paper introduces the lightweight precision detector series LYA-YOLO, a detecotor specifically designed for aerial imaging scenarios. Our approach comprises three core components: the Split-Interactive Fusion (SIF) module enhances spatio-semantic feature aggregation capabilities; the IIA-GhostC2f module combines Interleaved Interactive Attention with GhostDynamic convolution for efficient small object representation; while the LDCN-Head focuses on improving adaptive perception for multi-scale objects. Experiments on the VisDrone and UAVDT datasets demonstrate that LYA-YOLO achieves an outstanding accuracy-efficiency tradeoff: compared to existing real-time detectors, it boosts AP by 4% while reducing parameters and floating-point operations by over 70%. Extensive experiments confirm that LYA-YOLO delivers high accuracy, low latency, and extremely low resource consumption. Published: 2026-03-29T07:00:40+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.806 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Pengwei Wu; Yunfeng Xu; Yujie Ma; Yan Zhang; Yunling Xu

Journal: Expert Systems with Applications

DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2026.132166

Relevance: 0.806 (must_read)

Advancements in unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) have significantly expanded their application domains, encompassing urban surveillance, intelligent transportation, and infrastructure inspection. However, existing UAV-based object detection models still suffer from limitations such as low accuracy in small object detection, constrained real-time performance, and high computational costs. These constraints hinder their deployment capabilities on resource-constrained devices and their real-time detection abilities. To address these limitations, this paper introduces the lightweight precision detector series LYA-YOLO, a detecotor specifically designed for aerial imaging scenarios. Our approach comprises three core components: the Split-Interactive Fusion (SIF) module enhances spatio-semantic feature aggregation capabilities; the IIA-GhostC2f module combines Interleaved Interactive Attention with GhostDynamic convolution for efficient small object representation; while the LDCN-Head focuses on improving adaptive perception for multi-scale objects. Experiments on the VisDrone and UAVDT datasets demonstrate that LYA-YOLO achieves an outstanding accuracy-efficiency tradeoff: compared to existing real-time detectors, it boosts AP by 4% while reducing parameters and floating-point operations by over 70%. Extensive experiments confirm that LYA-YOLO delivers high accuracy, low latency, and extremely low resource consumption.

item.title: LG–BiFusion: Local and Global Bidirectional LiDAR–Camera Fusion for 3D Object Detection
item.dc:creator: Rongyun Zhang
Yuxiang Xu
Peicheng Shi
Ping Xiao
Rongxiang Wang
Hongwei Ou
item.description: Recently, Three-dimensional (3D) object detection by fusing light detection and ranging (LiDAR) and camera input has become a new trend. However, most mainstream methods only perform a one-way fusion of LiDAR and camera data, and do not fully utilise the interdependent characteristics of the two modalities. Simultaneously, the fusion of global LiDAR voxel features and camera features often lacks fine–grained regional information, leading to suboptimal results. Therefore, a local–global bidirectional and complementary LiDAR–camera fusion 3D object detection model is proposed in this study. Specifically, the LiDAR and camera modalities are fused in both directions at the global level. Through the camera–enhanced LiDAR and LiDAR–enhanced camera modules, rich voxel features with strong semantic information and camera features with enhanced spatial awareness are obtained. Subsequently, the foreground points and the camera features are locally fused using the 3D thermal value response to obtain fine–grained local foreground features. Finally, the three improved features are dynamically selected for object detection by the adaptive feature aggregation module. Extensive experiments were conducted on the nuScenes and KITTI datasets. Compared with the classical multimodal fusion model BEVFusion, the LG–BiFusion proposed in this study achieved a 3.6% increase in mean average precision (mAP) and a 3.2% increase in NDS on the nuScenes dataset, and improved the mAP of the multimodal model EPNet++ in the automotive category by 2.01% on the KITTI dataset. These experiments proved the effectiveness of the proposed model. Published: 2026-03-28T16:12:30+00:00 Venue: Knowledge-Based Systems Score: 0.793 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Rongyun Zhang; Yuxiang Xu; Peicheng Shi; Ping Xiao; Rongxiang Wang; Hongwei Ou

Journal: Knowledge-Based Systems

DOI: 10.1016/j.knosys.2026.115827

Relevance: 0.793 (must_read)

Recently, Three-dimensional (3D) object detection by fusing light detection and ranging (LiDAR) and camera input has become a new trend. However, most mainstream methods only perform a one-way fusion of LiDAR and camera data, and do not fully utilise the interdependent characteristics of the two modalities. Simultaneously, the fusion of global LiDAR voxel features and camera features often lacks fine–grained regional information, leading to suboptimal results. Therefore, a local–global bidirectional and complementary LiDAR–camera fusion 3D object detection model is proposed in this study. Specifically, the LiDAR and camera modalities are fused in both directions at the global level. Through the camera–enhanced LiDAR and LiDAR–enhanced camera modules, rich voxel features with strong semantic information and camera features with enhanced spatial awareness are obtained. Subsequently, the foreground points and the camera features are locally fused using the 3D thermal value response to obtain fine–grained local foreground features. Finally, the three improved features are dynamically selected for object detection by the adaptive feature aggregation module. Extensive experiments were conducted on the nuScenes and KITTI datasets. Compared with the classical multimodal fusion model BEVFusion, the LG–BiFusion proposed in this study achieved a 3.6% increase in mean average precision (mAP) and a 3.2% increase in NDS on the nuScenes dataset, and improved the mAP of the multimodal model EPNet++ in the automotive category by 2.01% on the KITTI dataset. These experiments proved the effectiveness of the proposed model.

item.title: Self-Adaptive Neural Networks for Domain Generalization in Medical Image Segmentation
item.dc:creator: Yan Wang
Zizhou Wang
Yangqin Feng
Lei Zhang
Rick Siow Mong Goh
Yong Liu
Liangli Zhen
item.description: Domain generalization is a critical challenge for expert systems in real-world medical environments, where data distributions often vary across acquisition settings. Existing domain generalization methods typically rely on carefully designed data augmentation or lack adaptivity to new data, which limits their applicability to new datasets. To address this problem, we propose a Self-Adaptive Neural Network (SA-Net) that learns domain-insensitive yet instance-adaptive feature representations. The proposed framework introduces a lightweight self-adaptive generator to rectify feature representations at the instance level and combines it with adversarial domain learning to suppress domain-specific information. Furthermore, a novel sample error loss is designed to improve the training of the self-adaptive generator by enforcing that removing the rectified latent representation from the original feature representation does not affect the final segmentation output. The effectiveness of SA-Net is validated on three representative medical image segmentation tasks, i.e., eye vessel segmentation, optic disc/cup segmentation, and skin lesion segmentation, across 12 datasets with substantial domain diversity. Experimental results demonstrate that SA-Net consistently outperforms state-of-the-art domain generalization methods on unseen domains while maintaining competitive computational efficiency. These results indicate that SA-Net provides a robust and scalable solution for intelligent medical image segmentation systems under distribution shifts. Published: 2026-03-29T20:24:39+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.792 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yan Wang; Zizhou Wang; Yangqin Feng; Lei Zhang; Rick Siow Mong Goh; Yong Liu; Liangli Zhen

Journal: Expert Systems with Applications

DOI: 10.1016/j.eswa.2026.132242

Relevance: 0.792 (must_read)

Domain generalization is a critical challenge for expert systems in real-world medical environments, where data distributions often vary across acquisition settings. Existing domain generalization methods typically rely on carefully designed data augmentation or lack adaptivity to new data, which limits their applicability to new datasets. To address this problem, we propose a Self-Adaptive Neural Network (SA-Net) that learns domain-insensitive yet instance-adaptive feature representations. The proposed framework introduces a lightweight self-adaptive generator to rectify feature representations at the instance level and combines it with adversarial domain learning to suppress domain-specific information. Furthermore, a novel sample error loss is designed to improve the training of the self-adaptive generator by enforcing that removing the rectified latent representation from the original feature representation does not affect the final segmentation output. The effectiveness of SA-Net is validated on three representative medical image segmentation tasks, i.e., eye vessel segmentation, optic disc/cup segmentation, and skin lesion segmentation, across 12 datasets with substantial domain diversity. Experimental results demonstrate that SA-Net consistently outperforms state-of-the-art domain generalization methods on unseen domains while maintaining competitive computational efficiency. These results indicate that SA-Net provides a robust and scalable solution for intelligent medical image segmentation systems under distribution shifts.

item.title: Finding Distributed Object-Centric Properties in Self-Supervised Transformers
item.dc:creator: Samyak Rawlekar
Amitabh Swain
Yujun Cai
Yiwei Wang
Ming-Hsuan Yang
Narendra Ahuja
item.description: Self-supervised Vision Transformers (ViTs) like DINO show an emergent ability to discover objects, typically observed in [CLS] token attention maps of the final layer. However, these maps often contain spurious activations resulting in poor localization of objects. This is because the [CLS] token, trained on an image-level objective, summarizes the entire image instead of focusing on objects. This aggregation dilutes the object-centric information existing in the local, patch-level interactions. We analyze this by computing inter-patch similarity using patch-level attention components (query, key, and value) across all layers. We find that: (1) Object-centric properties are encoded in the similarity maps derived from all three components ($q, k, v$), unlike prior work that uses only key features or the [CLS] token. (2) This object-centric information is distributed across the network, not just confined to the final layer. Based on these insights, we introduce Object-DINO, a training-free method that extracts this distributed object-centric information. Object-DINO clusters attention heads across all layers based on the similarities of their patches and automatically identifies the object-centric cluster corresponding to all objects. We demonstrate Object-DINO's effectiveness on two applications: enhancing unsupervised object discovery (+3.6 to +12.4 CorLoc gains) and mitigating object hallucination in Multimodal Large Language Models by providing visual grounding. Our results demonstrate that using this distributed object-centric information improves downstream tasks without additional training. Published: 2026-03-27T07:22:04+00:00 Venue: arXiv Score: 0.791 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Samyak Rawlekar; Amitabh Swain; Yujun Cai; Yiwei Wang; Ming-Hsuan Yang; Narendra Ahuja

Journal: arXiv

Relevance: 0.791 (must_read)

Self-supervised Vision Transformers (ViTs) like DINO show an emergent ability to discover objects, typically observed in [CLS] token attention maps of the final layer. However, these maps often contain spurious activations resulting in poor localization of objects. This is because the [CLS] token, trained on an image-level objective, summarizes the entire image instead of focusing on objects. This aggregation dilutes the object-centric information existing in the local, patch-level interactions. We analyze this by computing inter-patch similarity using patch-level attention components (query, key, and value) across all layers. We find that: (1) Object-centric properties are encoded in the similarity maps derived from all three components ($q, k, v$), unlike prior work that uses only key features or the [CLS] token. (2) This object-centric information is distributed across the network, not just confined to the final layer. Based on these insights, we introduce Object-DINO, a training-free method that extracts this distributed object-centric information. Object-DINO clusters attention heads across all layers based on the similarities of their patches and automatically identifies the object-centric cluster corresponding to all objects. We demonstrate Object-DINO's effectiveness on two applications: enhancing unsupervised object discovery (+3.6 to +12.4 CorLoc gains) and mitigating object hallucination in Multimodal Large Language Models by providing visual grounding. Our results demonstrate that using this distributed object-centric information improves downstream tasks without additional training.

item.title: Towards Efficient Language Giants: A Comprehensive Survey on Structural Optimizations and Compression Techniques for Large Language Models
item.dc:creator: Gilhyeon Lee
Seonggeun Kim
Dongjun Lee
Kyungmin Goh
Hyun Kim
item.description: The impressive success of large language models (LLMs) across a broad spectrum of NLP tasks has attracted considerable attention in both academia and industry. However, their inference incurs substantial computational and memory overhead, making it challenging to efficiently deploy them in latency-sensitive or resource-constrained environments. This survey provides a comprehensive overview of model-level optimization techniques developed to address these issues. Specifically, we examine efficient module designs for core Transformer components–including multi-head attention, feed-forward networks, normalization, and positional encoding–and summarize representative compression methods such as quantization, pruning, knowledge distillation, approximation, and parameter sharing. We categorize recent advances in each area, analyzing their algorithmic principles, empirical trade-offs, and impact on inference efficiency. Furthermore, we propose practical design strategies that effectively combine efficient modules with compression techniques to significantly reduce inference costs while meeting application-specific accuracy and latency requirements. Published: 2026-03-28T16:11:01+00:00 Venue: Neural Networks Score: 0.791 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Gilhyeon Lee; Seonggeun Kim; Dongjun Lee; Kyungmin Goh; Hyun Kim

Journal: Neural Networks

DOI: 10.1016/j.neunet.2026.108900

Relevance: 0.791 (must_read)

The impressive success of large language models (LLMs) across a broad spectrum of NLP tasks has attracted considerable attention in both academia and industry. However, their inference incurs substantial computational and memory overhead, making it challenging to efficiently deploy them in latency-sensitive or resource-constrained environments. This survey provides a comprehensive overview of model-level optimization techniques developed to address these issues. Specifically, we examine efficient module designs for core Transformer components–including multi-head attention, feed-forward networks, normalization, and positional encoding–and summarize representative compression methods such as quantization, pruning, knowledge distillation, approximation, and parameter sharing. We categorize recent advances in each area, analyzing their algorithmic principles, empirical trade-offs, and impact on inference efficiency. Furthermore, we propose practical design strategies that effectively combine efficient modules with compression techniques to significantly reduce inference costs while meeting application-specific accuracy and latency requirements.

item.title: Geometry-Guided Multi-View 3D Object Detection for Autonomous Driving
item.dc:creator: Zefeng Pan
Huifeng Wang
Hao Du
Xiaowei Zhang
item.description: Recently, camera-based Bird’s Eye View (BEV) perception has played an increasingly important role in autonomous driving. However, existing LSS-based methods largely rely on 2D semantic cues to infer depth and geometry indirectly. They lack explicit modeling of 3D geometric structure, which limits both depth reconstruction and the geometric awareness of BEV representations. In this paper, we incorporate surface normals as an interpretable 3D geometric prior within the BEV framework, and proposed a novel geometry-guided approach for BEV perception. Based on a virtual sub-pixel local planar assumption and an analytic normal-depth relationship, we design a Normal-To-Depth Module. It reconstructs fine-grained depth geometry in a low-resolution space at a low cost, thereby guiding the estimation of discrete depth distributions. We further propose a plug-in, parameter-free Local Planar Depth Aggregation (LPDA) module that explicitly models local planar geometric consistency to aggregate discrete depth, alleviating depth holes and errors caused by sparse supervision. In addition, we design a Deformable Multi-Task (DMT) Head with cross-task deformable sampling interaction for joint learning. Together, these designs yields more accurate initial normal and depth predictions, and enhance subsequent geometric correction as well as the quality of BEV representation. On the nuScenes benchmark, our method achieves 61.9% NDS. Extensive experiments and ablations validate the effectiveness of the proposed designs, demonstrating the benefits of integrating normal-based geometric cues for BEV 3D detection. Published: 2026-03-29T23:26:09+00:00 Venue: Knowledge-Based Systems Score: 0.789 (must_read)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Zefeng Pan; Huifeng Wang; Hao Du; Xiaowei Zhang

Journal: Knowledge-Based Systems

DOI: 10.1016/j.knosys.2026.115901

Relevance: 0.789 (must_read)

Recently, camera-based Bird’s Eye View (BEV) perception has played an increasingly important role in autonomous driving. However, existing LSS-based methods largely rely on 2D semantic cues to infer depth and geometry indirectly. They lack explicit modeling of 3D geometric structure, which limits both depth reconstruction and the geometric awareness of BEV representations. In this paper, we incorporate surface normals as an interpretable 3D geometric prior within the BEV framework, and proposed a novel geometry-guided approach for BEV perception. Based on a virtual sub-pixel local planar assumption and an analytic normal-depth relationship, we design a Normal-To-Depth Module. It reconstructs fine-grained depth geometry in a low-resolution space at a low cost, thereby guiding the estimation of discrete depth distributions. We further propose a plug-in, parameter-free Local Planar Depth Aggregation (LPDA) module that explicitly models local planar geometric consistency to aggregate discrete depth, alleviating depth holes and errors caused by sparse supervision. In addition, we design a Deformable Multi-Task (DMT) Head with cross-task deformable sampling interaction for joint learning. Together, these designs yields more accurate initial normal and depth predictions, and enhance subsequent geometric correction as well as the quality of BEV representation. On the nuScenes benchmark, our method achieves 61.9% NDS. Extensive experiments and ablations validate the effectiveness of the proposed designs, demonstrating the benefits of integrating normal-based geometric cues for BEV 3D detection.

item.title: Robust panoramic multi-object tracking with category-aware data association and adaptive noise estimation for unmanned surface vehicles
item.dc:creator: Zhilin Yang
Yong Yin
Qianfeng Jing
Zeyuan Shao
Zhengshan Wang
Haitong Xu
C.Guedes Soares
item.description: Unmanned Surface Vehicle (USV) 360° panoramic video enables comprehensive perception of maritime objects. However, multi-object tracking in USV panoramic video remains challenging due to category differences, wave disturbances, and appearance degradation caused by panoramic stitching, which often undermine data association stability and motion prediction accuracy. To address these issues, this paper proposes USVTrack360, a Tracking-by-Detection framework designed for maritime panoramic tracking. A dynamic-head-enhanced You Only Look Once version 12 (YOLOv12) detector is first adopted to provide stable localisation and classification results for subsequent tracking. Then, a Category-Aware Cascaded Matching (CACM) strategy partitions objects into vessels and navigational aids and performs motion-driven association with category consistency constraints, reducing identity instability caused by category differences and degraded appearance features. In addition, a Dual-Adaptive Noise Kalman Filter (DANKF) dynamically adjusts process and observation noise based on motion matching cost and detection confidence, improving robustness to object non-linear motion and platform disturbances. Experiments on the 360-Pohang dataset demonstrate that USVTrack360 achieves 74.31 Higher Order Tracking Accuracy (HOTA), 73.88 Multiple Object Tracking Accuracy (MOTA), and 83.66 Identity F1 Score (IDF1) at 43.3 Frames Per Second (FPS) on a single RTX 4070 GPU, outperforming the baseline ByteTrack by 1.8%, 0.6%, and 1.4% in HOTA, MOTA, and IDF1, respectively, and reducing identity switches by 66.3%. These results indicate that USVTrack360 enhances association reliability and trajectory stability in complex maritime panoramic environments while maintaining real-time performance, providing an effective multi-object tracking solution for USV panoramic visual perception systems. Published: 2026-03-29T23:26:16+00:00 Venue: Expert Systems with Applications Score: 0.788 (must_read)
